title: Fall 2024 Presidents Office Program Review (Part 2)
text:
3 | P a g e
1. DESCRIPTION OF PROGRAM
a) College Mission
We are a comprehensive community college serving the economic, educational, lifelong learning, and
workforce needs of Siskiyou County and the other communities our programs reach. College of the
Siskiyous provides students a strong education leading to associate degrees, certificates, college
transfer, career and technical education, workforce training, and basic skills preparation. We
understand the transformative power of higher education and seek to inspire students by offering
inclusive and practical learning experiences that are relevant in today’s global economy and
environment. We strive to prepare our students to align their newly-gained skills and experiences with
those best suited to the work they wish to pursue and the lives they wish to lead. At the same time, we
aim to inspire our students to engage actively, question deeply, reason critically, think boldly,
communicate effectively, act innovatively, work collaboratively, and live ethically.
The President’s Office operations are directly aligned with the College mission. The
Superintendent/President ensures compliance of accreditation standard 1 by affirming that:
1.1. The institution has established a clearly defined mission that appropriately reflects its
character, values, structure, and unique student demographics. The institution’s
mission articulates its commitment to ensuring equitable educational opportunities
and outcomes for all students.
1.2. The institution establishes meaningful and ambitious goals for institutional
improvement, innovation, and equitable student outcomes.
1.3. The institution holds itself accountable for achieving its mission and goals and
regularly reviews relevant, meaningfully disaggregated data to evaluate its progress
and inform plans for continued improvement and innovation.

-- 3 of 17 --

title: Fall 2024 Presidents Office Program Review (Part 3)
text:
4 | P a g e
1.4. The institution’s mission directs resource allocation, innovation, and continuous
quality improvement through ongoing systematic planning and evaluation of
programs and services.
1.5. The institution regularly communicates progress toward achieving its mission and
goals with internal and external stakeholders in order to promote understanding of
institutional strengths, priorities, and areas for continued improvement. (ER 19)
b) Organizational structure and staffing
Administration
• Dr. Char Perlas; Superintendent / President
o Deborah Goltz; Executive Assistant II, President & Board
o VACANT; Vice President, Administrative Services
o Patrick Walton; Vice President, Student Services
o Dr. Mark Fields; Vice President, Academic Affairs
o John Tarantino; Director, Planning, Assessment & Research
o Dawnie Slabaugh; Director, Public Relations & College
Foundation
o Samantha Worthington; Director, FIELD Manager ISA
o Kristi Wilson; Director, Human Resources
c) Who We Serve
The President’s Office responds to a variety of stakeholders within and outside of the local
community. We address inquiries, such as complaints, public records requests, report
requirements and concerns in a timely manner. The Superintendent/President also represents
the College and District by serving on a number of local organizations in which progress
towards achieving the College's mission and goals is shared:

-- 4 of 17 --

5 | P a g e
• American Association of University Women
• Complete College America
• Cradle to Career 4 Siskiyous
• Foundation Board
• LOLA (League of Local Agencies)
• North State Together
• Rotary Clubs of Siskiyou County (Weed, Montague, Mount Shasta)
The Superintendent/President also serves on the following organizations representing the
College and the District nationally:
• Accrediting Commission for Community and Junior Colleges (ACCJC) – Peer Review
Team Chair
• Association of California Community College Administrators (ACCCA) – Executive
Board Member and chair of the Communications and Marketing Committee
• CCCCO LGBTQ+ Advisory Committee – Member
• California Community College Chancellor’s Office (CCCCO) Telecommunications &
Technology Advisory Committee – Co-Chair
• CEO California Community College (CEOCCC) Board – Vice President of the North
and co-chair for the Racial Equity Inclusive Excellence (REIE) Taskforce
• Higher Education Research & Development Institute (HERDI) – Board Member
d) Brief Description of Services
The President’s Office and its Superintendent/President is responsible for overseeing the
entire College, including the planning and budgeting process, academic programs, student
and administrative services, institutional advancement, and human resources. The
Superintendent/President ensures that the College meets the educational needs of its
students and the community. The Superintendent/President and members of the President’s
Office represent the College to local businesses and industry, other educational institutions,
federal, state, and local agencies, and potential donors to the COS Foundation.

-- 5 of 17 --

title: Fall 2024 Presidents Office Program Review (Part 4)
text:
6 | P a g e
Trend data, associated with the President’s Office, would encompass the following three
main divisions:
1. Academic Affairs
2. Administrative Services
3. Student Services
Data collected would be based on the President’s Office Service Area Outcomes (SAO) which
are outlined in section 3.
e) Operations as percent of the college’s budget
The President’s Office consists of three main budgets:
1. Board of Trustees
2. CEO
3. Economic Development
The 24-25 Adopted Budget for each area are as follows:
• Board of Trustees = $150,464.00
• CEO = $413,918.00
• Economic Development = $500
Total President’s Office budget = $565,064.00
The majority of the budget reflects salaries and benefits and is 2.23% of the District’s 24-25
budget.

-- 6 of 17 --

7 | P a g e
2. EXTERNAL FACTORS WITH
SIGNIFICANT IMPACT
a) Budgetary Constraints or Opportunities
The Superintendent/President is responsible for the District-wide budget which, in the past,
has struggled with 50% law compliance. Thankfully, due to hiring new FT Instructional
Faculty, increase in instructional spending associated with Instructional Service Agreements
and augmented instructional classified salaries, reinstatement of Fund 61 and coding
adjustments, the District is in compliance with the 50% law for AY 23-24 and has reported
53.41% on its 311.
Looking forward, the District needs an additional 150 FTES to ‘break out of hold harmless’.
Once this goal has been met, the District can focus efforts on long-term stability. In the
meantime, to maintain the District’s fiscal health, strategic financial decision-making and
creative budgeting will need to be executed.
b) Competition from other institutions
The physical location of the College makes it the primary higher education institution in
Siskiyou County. Although the College is the only option for local students, College of the
Siskiyous is also on the California Virtual Campus (CVC) exchange which creates online
competition among all California Community Colleges in the CVC exchange.
Luckily, as a result of our robust online courses, reputable online support services and access
to online resources, the College has succeeded in increasing its online enrollments.
c) Job market
Due to the rural location of College of the Siskiyous, the local job market is limited. The
College currently offers certificate and degree programs in leading sectors with the north/far
north region: Nursing, Emergency Medical Services, Administration of Justice Fire
Technology and Business.

-- 7 of 17 --

title: Fall 2024 Presidents Office Program Review (Part 5)
text:
8 | P a g e
3.PROGRESS ON SERVICE AREA
OUTCOMES (SAO) ASSESSMENTS
a) Please summarize Service Area Outcome (SAO) assessment results.
Include a discussion of whether the program met its target for each
SAO.
The 2024 Comprehensive Program Review for the President’s Office is the office’s initial
program review. As such, the following Service Area Outcomes have been established:
1. Academic Excellence:
• Outcome: Increased student retention and graduation rates.
• Strategies: Implementing academic support programs, improving faculty
development, and enhancing curriculum relevance.
2. Student Success:
• Outcome: Improved student satisfaction and engagement.
• Strategies: Expanding student services, fostering a positive campus culture, and
promoting student involvement in extracurricular activities.
3. Fiscal Responsibility:
• Outcome: Sustainable financial operations and responsible resource allocation.
• Strategies: Developing a comprehensive budget plan, exploring funding
opportunities, and implementing cost-saving measures.
4. Community Engagement:
• Outcome: Strengthened partnerships with local businesses, organizations, and
government agencies.
• Strategies: Collaborating on community projects, hosting public events, and
promoting the college's role in regional development.
5. Faculty and Staff Development:
• Outcome: A highly skilled and motivated workforce.
• Strategies: Providing professional development opportunities, supporting faculty
research, and promoting a positive work environment.
6. Institutional Advancement:
• Outcome: Increased alumni engagement, fundraising efforts, and public relations.
• Strategies: Developing a strategic fundraising plan, strengthening alumni networks,
and enhancing the college's public image.

-- 8 of 17 --

title: Fall 2024 Presidents Office Program Review (Part 8)
text:
11 | P a g e
4. VISION
a) Unit’s Vision
Vision: To be a nationally recognized institution of higher learning that empowers students to
achieve their full potential, contributes to the economic vitality of the region, and fosters a
culture of innovation and lifelong learning.
Key goals to achieve within the next four years:
Student Success:
• Increase student retention and graduation rates by 10%.
• Expand access to affordable, high-quality education for underserved populations.
• Provide students with the skills and knowledge needed to succeed in a rapidly
changing job market (specifically keeping AI at the forefront).
Academic Excellence:
• Enhance curriculum relevance and innovation to meet the evolving needs of students
and employers. Expand Baccalaureate degree offerings.
• Recruit and retain top-quality faculty and staff.
• Foster a culture of collegiality, scholarship, and creative activity.
Community Engagement:
• Strengthen partnerships with local businesses, organizations, universities and
government agencies.
• Contribute to the economic development and cultural vitality of the region.
• Promote civic engagement and social responsibility among students.
Institutional Advancement:
• Increase alumni engagement and fundraising efforts.
• Enhance the college's public image and reputation.
• Secure sustainable funding sources to support the college's mission.

-- 11 of 17 --

12 | P a g e
Sustainability:
• Incorporate sustainability principles into all aspects of campus operations.
• Educate students about environmental issues and promote sustainable practices.
• Reduce the college's environmental footprint.
By focusing on these goals, the College of the Siskiyous President's Office can position the
institution for continued growth and success in the years to come.
b) Alignment with the College Vision
The President’s Office Vision and Goals directly align with the College’s Vision:
‘Transforming students’ lives through lifelong learning and success.’
Efforts towards achieving the key goals will have a direct impact on ‘transforming students’
lives through lifelong learning and success’. Each of the key goals are also aligned with the
President’s Office Service Area Outcomes (SAO).
5. Four-Year Action Plan
The following four-year action plan includes specific activities, collaborative area, method of
assessment, identified resources, timelines and associated SAO. The President’s Office will
work with each collaborative area in achieving indicated activities.

-- 12 of 17 --

-- 16 of 17 --

17 | P a g e
6. Conclusion
The President’s Office is responsible for the entire operations of the District and College.
With the recent turnover in staff resulting from the retirement incentive followed by
COVID, the President’s Office has taken initiative on numerous reports and activities
related to Accreditation, Administrative Services and Human Resources.
A selection of recent achievements include:
• Awarding of a 32 million dollar grant to create a new student housing complex
• Achieving compliance with the 50% law
• A demonstrated 41.9% increase in enrollment from Academic Year 22-23 to 23-24
• Establishment of the Strategic Enrollment Management Plan and successful
achievement of stated annual objectives
• Creation of a comprehensive Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO) Plan and
successful implementation of activities
• Eligibility to offer an inaugural Bachelorette of Science in Paramedicine
• Selection and participation in the Common Cloud Data Platform Pilot
The President’s Office is eager to implement this Noninstructional Program Review plan. We
anticipate that this plan will have a significant and beneficial impact on our students and the
broader community.

-- 17 of 17 --
